essence: An asset-heavy, high-fixed-cost business: build the network once, then every extra subscriber is almost pure margin - scale wins.
Spectrum is a scarce, government-controlled input that can make or break the economics.
The product is commoditising (voice/SMS dead, data cheap) - the game is moving 'up the stack' to digital services.
It's a 3-4 player oligopoly where one price move by the leader can reset the entire market overnight.
The two numbers that matter most: ARPU (revenue per user) and churn (users lost).

definition.oneLiner: Businesses that build and run networks to carry voice and data, selling connectivity to consumers and enterprises.
definition.jobToBeDone: Keep people and machines connected - reliable, fast, always-on access to communication and the internet.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit concentrates where scale + customer ownership sit - operators with the most subscribers. Towers are steadier but lower-margin; devices/content are thin or loss-leaders.
valueChain.powerShift: Value is migrating from 'minutes & messages' (commoditised) -> data -> digital services bundled on top (content, fintech, enterprise cloud).

variants: Prepaid (mass-market, pay-as-you-go) vs Postpaid (premium, billed monthly)
Wholesale / infrastructure (lease towers & spectrum to others)

unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Subscribers x ARPU. ARPU rises via tariff hikes, data upsell, postpaid mix, and bundles.
keyKPIs: Churn - % of subscribers lost per period

costStructure.shape: Very high fixed cost (network, spectrum, capex), low marginal cost per extra user -> big operating leverage.
costStructure.leverage: Once the network is built, each new subscriber is almost pure margin - so scale and capacity utilisation are everything.

moats: Scale economics (high fixed cost favours big players)

structuralForces.regulation: Heavy - spectrum auctions, licence fees, pricing oversight, security rules. Often the biggest swing factor.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Capex-cyclical around tech generations (3G->4G->5G): big investment waves, then harvest.
structuralForces.substitution: OTT apps (WhatsApp etc.) cannibalised voice & SMS; telcos risk becoming 'dumb pipes'.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Few equipment vendors (Ericsson, Nokia, Huawei, Samsung) hold some power; governments control spectrum (huge power).

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium - telcos are active adopters in networks & customer ops, still early on monetisation.
keyUseCases: Network optimisation & predictive maintenance (cut downtime, capex efficiency)
AI customer service (chat/voice bots) - a large cost pool
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: Biggest near-term value in network ops (capex/opex) and customer care (largest headcount cost). Longer-term: telcos as AI/edge-compute infrastructure providers.
threatsDisruption: Commoditisation if telco stays just the pipe

ranges: Net debt / EBITDA: ~2-4x typical, higher post-spectrum auctions
Leader subscriber share in a consolidated market: ~35-40%
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

valueLevers: Raise ARPU (tariff hikes, data upsell, postpaid mix, bundles)
Capex & network efficiency (ROI per site, infra sharing)

keyTerms.ARPU: Average Revenue Per User - monthly revenue / subscribers; the core monetisation metric.
keyTerms.Churn: Share of subscribers who leave in a period; high churn erodes the base.
keyTerms.MNP: Mobile Number Portability - keep your number when switching operators; lowered switching costs.
keyTerms.Spectrum: Licensed radio frequencies needed to run a network; bought at government auctions.
keyTerms.AGR: Adjusted Gross Revenue - the base on which Indian telcos owe government dues; a major legacy liability.
keyTerms.OTT: Over-The-Top apps (WhatsApp, YouTube) that ride on the telco's network and bypass its services.
keyTerms.5G SA: 5G Standalone - a full 5G core network (vs riding on 4G), enabling advanced low-latency use cases.
keyTerms.Tenancy ratio: Average tenants per tower; higher = better tower economics.

commonCaseTypes: Monetising digital services (move up the stack)
Cost transformation in a price war

questionsToAskFirst: Is the problem price (ARPU) or volume (subscribers)?
Where are they in the capex cycle - investing or harvesting?
Prepaid vs postpaid mix, and the trend?
What's churn, and what's driving it?
How does network quality rank vs competitors?
Any regulatory / spectrum event on the horizon?
rookieTraps: Chasing subscriber growth while ARPU/quality erodes
Ignoring the spectrum/capex cycle when judging margins
Treating telecom as just a pipe and missing the ecosystem play

whatsUniqueHere: Telecom is where natural-monopoly economics meets brutal price competition. Spectrum is a government-controlled input that can make or break the business, and a single tariff move by the price leader can reset the whole market overnight.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: 3 private telcos + state-owned BSNL/MTNL; consolidated from ~12 players after the post-2016 price war.
demandDrivers: World's cheapest data + huge youth base
structuralChallenges: Rural economics hard to serve profitably
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Airlines - high fixed cost, perishable capacity, price wars

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India: 3 private players + BSNL. Jio (~470M subs, #1), Airtel (~390M, higher ARPU), Vi (distant 3rd, struggling).
